data: Abel DIDOUH Radio Frequency (RF) Engineer LinkedIn

Welcome to this series of articles dedicated to Software-Defined Radio (SDR). SDR is a technology that transforms how radio systems are designed and deployed. Traditionally, radios were built with specific hardware components for each signal processing function (amplification, modulation, demodulation, etc.). In contrast, in an SDR, much of this processing is performed using software, enabling greater flexibility, reusability, and adaptability.
data.commercial-and-military-rise-2000s#Comme:
In the 2000s, SDR increasingly became a reality in commercial and military domains. The military, in particular, saw SDR as a means to reduce complexity and costs of communication systems. It allowed for rapid adaptation of communication frequencies and protocols without having to replace hardware, especially in warfare environments where flexibility is crucial.
Software Defined Radio enables enhanced military communications - Military Embedded Systems In 2000, companies like National Instruments and Ettus Research began developing accessible SDR platforms, such as the NI-USRP (Universal Software Radio Peripheral) and FlexRadio systems, which facilitated the transition to SDR implementations in contexts as varied as wireless networks, research, and radio signal monitoring.
data.early-explorations-1960s---1970s#Early E:
The concept of software-defined radio dates back to the 1960s-1970s, but it wasn’t until the rise of computers and digital technologies that the idea truly began to take shape. The idea of “programmable radio” or adaptable radio emerged when researchers began exploring ways to process signals using software.
At that time, early radios were primarily analog. The need for more flexible radios capable of processing different types of signals became evident in telecommunications and military domains, which sought systems capable of adapting to different types of modulation and frequencies.
data.in-conclusion#In Conclusion: The history of software-defined radio is marked by a series of technological breakthroughs in digital signal processing. From the first research in the 1960s to democratization via open-source platforms in the 2010s, SDR has radically changed how communication systems are designed and used. Thanks to pioneers like Joseph Mitola and early implementations in the military domain, SDR is now a key element of modern telecommunications.

data.series-plan-discovering-software-defined: Introduction to Software-Defined Radio Theoretical foundations of radio Analog and heterodyne architectures Signal digitization technology IQ architecture: foundation of digital processing in SDR Digital processing chains in an SDR Temporal and frequency synchronization Digital modulations and demodulations SDR tools Let’s dive into the world of software-defined radios.
data.the-birth-of-modern-sdr-1980s-1990s#The:
In the 1980s, the advancement of digital signal processors (DSP) enabled more advanced digital signal processing, laying the foundation for modern SDR. Research intensified around digital modulation, with researchers beginning to explore the possibility of replacing specific hardware components with software, particularly for radio signal processing.
The term “Software Defined Radio” was introduced in 1984 by Joseph Mitola. In 1992, he published his seminal article describing SDR as a radio that uses software to perform signal processing functions that were previously done by hardware components.
SDR chronological timeline
data.the-era-of-democratization-and-open-sour:
The 2010s marked a true democratization of SDR. Open-source platforms like GNU Radio, SDR# (SDRSharp), or HDSDR made SDR technologies accessible to a broad audience, from hobbyists to researchers. These software platforms and tools allowed users to build their own SDR equipment at relatively low cost, transforming how researchers, engineers, and hobbyists use radio technologies.
The emergence of Raspberry Pi and affordable hardware components like RTL-SDR enabled entry into the SDR world at very competitive prices, thus opening the door to a new generation of radio amateurs and innovators.

data: In this section, we will explore the fundamental concepts of radio, essential for understanding how software-defined radios (SDR) work. By addressing notions such as baseband, modulations and channel capacity, we will lay the foundations necessary to understand the technical challenges that SDRs address.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Baseband Modulations Channel Capacity (Shannon) Noise Filtering in SDR
data.baseband#Baseband: A baseband signal refers to an original signal, as it is produced by the source, before any transformation for transmission. For example, a digital audio file is a baseband signal: its spectrum is centered around zero frequency and extends to a maximum frequency related to its content (a few KHz for voice).
data.baseband-filtering#Baseband filtering:
Filtering a baseband signal is essential for several reasons in communication systems. It mainly helps reduce inter-symbol interference. This filtering also limits the signal bandwidth, which helps avoid spectral waste and comply with frequency regulations imposed by authorities.
Thus, baseband filtering is an essential concept in the field of software-defined radio. Among the most commonly used types of filters we find:
Gaussian filter Raised cosine filter Matched filters Each of these filters presents specific advantages and challenges, depending on the application context. While in traditional radio systems, filtering is performed with analog components, in SDR, filters are implemented digitally. This allows increased flexibility, as it is possible to modify filter characteristics simply by programming, without needing to change hardware.
data.conclusion#Conclusion: Software-defined radio (SDR) relies on fundamental concepts such as baseband signal, modulation, noise management, and filtering, which enable the design of flexible and efficient communication systems. Shannon’s information theory provides a theoretical framework for optimizing communication channel capacity, while advances in digital signal processing have allowed SDR to revolutionize telecommunications. By combining techniques such as digital modulation and baseband filtering, SDR offers unprecedented adaptability, making communication systems more robust against interference and constraints of modern environments.
data.modulations#Modulations:
To transmit this signal over a physical medium (cable, fiber, radio waves), it is often necessary to modulate the signal. Modulation consists of “carrying” the information on a higher carrier frequency in order to:
adapt the signal to the transmission channel, allow simultaneous transmission of multiple signals (multiplexing), reduce interference in certain media. There are several types of modulations:
Amplitude Modulation (AM): the amplitude of the carrier varies according to the signal to be transmitted. Frequency Modulation (FM): the frequency of the carrier varies according to the signal. Phase Modulation (PM): the phase of the carrier changes to encode the information.
data.noise#Noise:
Noise is any unwanted disturbance that corrupts a radio signal. It can come from natural sources (such as heat in electronic circuits, called thermal noise) or artificial sources (interference from other devices, for example a microwave near a Wi-Fi router). We have seen above that noise limits the capacity of a channel by making signals more difficult to decode.
Noise, in a traditional system, can be managed by amplifiers and analog components. In SDR, noise is often reduced by digital algorithms that dynamically adapt signal processing. This allows maintaining optimal signal quality in noisy environments.

Proakis, J. G., & Salehi, M. (2008). Digital Communications. 5th Edition, McGraw-Hill. Mitola, J. (1995). The Software Radio Architecture. IEEE Communications Magazine, 33(5), 26–38. Couch, L. W. data.shannon-and-channel-capacity#Shannon and:
In 1948, Claude Edwood Shannon modeled for the first time a communication channel scheme in his article entitled: A Mathematical Theory of Communication. All stakeholders in signal transmission are characterized from the information source to its destination.
Communication channel diagram The scientist is a pioneer of information theory. He established a fundamental theorem that defines the capacity of a channel.
The formula that models this phenomenon is as follows:
C=BWlog⁡2(1+SN) C = BW \log_{2}{(1+\frac{S}{N})} C=BWlog2​(1+NS​) C: Channel capacity (bit/s) BW: Bandwidth (Hz) S/N: Signal-to-noise ratio (unitless, on linear scale) Shannon’s formula allows calculating the maximum capacity of a channel, that is, the amount of information it can transmit per second. The capacity depends on two important factors:
bandwidth signal-to-noise ratio In practice, this formula helps us understand how to maximize information transmission in systems such as wireless networks.
Let’s take an example to manipulate Shannon’s formula.
Example - Theoretical capacity vs real throughput (Wi-Fi case)
Let’s imagine a Wi-Fi network:
Available bandwidth: B = 20 MHz Measured signal-to-noise ratio: S/N = 25 dB S/N ratio conversion: SN=102510=102.5≈316.23 \frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{25}{10}} = 10^{2.5} \approx 316.23 NS​=101025​=102.5≈316.23 Shannon’s formula: C=BWlog⁡2(1+SN) C = BW \log_{2}{(1+\frac{S}{N})} C=BWlog2​(1+NS​) C=20⋅106log⁡2(1+316.23) C = 20 \cdot 10^{6} \log_{2}{(1+316.23)} C=20⋅106log2​(1+316.23) C≈20⋅106⋅8.31≈166.2Mbit/s C \approx 20 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 8.31 \approx 166.2 Mbit/s C≈20⋅106⋅8.31≈166.2Mbit/sThe theoretical capacity is approximately equal to 166 Mb/s. While the real throughput is often between 80 and 120 Mb/s. This is due to losses, interference, management protocols and collisions. Thus, this shows that in practice, the obtained throughput is always lower than the maximum capacity of a channel.

data: When we talk about radio communication, we often imagine an antenna capturing invisible waves that carry music, conversations or data. But behind this image lies an entire electronic chain. In this article, we will explore one of the most used architectures in radio signal processing: the heterodyne.
data.advantages-of-heterodyne-architecture#Ad:
The heterodyne architecture offers several significant advantages:
Increased selectivity: Filtering at the intermediate frequency allows precise isolation of the desired frequency band, reducing interference from neighboring signals. Improved sensitivity: The LNA and IF amplifier allow capturing and processing very weak signals with a good signal-to-noise ratio. Flexibility: By adjusting the local oscillator frequency, the receiver can cover a wide range of RF frequencies without modifying the IF chain components. Stability: Circuits operating at a lower intermediate frequency are less sensitive to noise and temperature variations, which improves reliability. Compatibility with complex modulation: Heterodyne facilitates processing of modern modulations like QPSK or OFDM, common in digital communications. However, the heterodyne architecture also presents challenges, such as the problem of image frequencies (unwanted signals at fLO+fIF f_{LO} + f_{IF} fLO​+fIF​) which require high-performance RF filters to be eliminated.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Basic components of an RF chain Principle of heterodyne in radio Basic components in a heterodyne architecture Advantages of heterodyne architecture
data.basic-components-of-an-rf-chain#Basic co:
An RF chain, for radio frequency, is an electronic system designed to capture, process and transmit radio signals in a frequency range generally ranging from a few kilohertz to several gigahertz. It consists of a set of components that work in synergy to capture, amplify, filter, convert and transmit a radio signal. Here are the main elements of an RF chain:
Antenna: The antenna captures electromagnetic waves for reception or emits them for transmission. It is designed to function efficiently in a specific frequency band, determined by its geometry and materials. RF Filter: This component selects the desired frequency band while eliminating parasitic or out-of-band signals, thus reducing interference. Low Noise Amplifier (LNA): In reception, the LNA amplifies the weak signal captured by the antenna while minimizing added noise, thus preserving the signal-to-noise ratio. Mixer: It combines the RF signal with a reference signal generated by a local oscillator (LO) to convert the RF frequency to an intermediate frequency (IF) that is easier to process. Local Oscillator (LO): This component generates a stable signal at a precise frequency, used by the mixer to perform frequency conversion. Power Amplifier: In transmission, this amplifier increases the signal power before its transmission via the antenna, ensuring sufficient range. Analog-to-Digital (ADC) and Digital-to-Analog (DAC) Converters: These components ensure the transition between analog and digital signals in modern systems, such as software-defined radios (SDR), where digital processing is preferred. In modern systems, additional components such as surface acoustic wave (SAW) filters or frequency synthesizers based on phase-locked loops (PLL) can be integrated to improve performance and stability.
Diagram of an RF chain in transmission
data.components-specific-to-heterodyne-archit:
In a heterodyne architecture, the signal path follows a precise sequence:
Antenna and RF filter: The antenna captures the signal, which is then filtered to isolate the desired frequency band. Low Noise Amplifier (LNA): The filtered signal is amplified to increase its level while minimizing added noise. Mixer: The RF signal is multiplied with the local oscillator signal, producing an intermediate frequency (IF). Local Oscillator (LO): Provides a stable signal for the mixer. Modern oscillators often use PLLs to guarantee high precision. IF Filter: This filter, often narrower than the initial RF filter, precisely isolates the intermediate frequency, eliminating unwanted frequencies from the mixing. IF Amplifier: Amplifies the IF signal to prepare it for demodulation. Demodulator: Extracts useful information (audio, data, etc.) from the IF signal. Depending on the type of modulation (AM, FM, QAM, etc.), the demodulator may vary. In some architectures, such as superheterodyne, multiple frequency conversion stages (double or triple heterodyne) are used to further improve selectivity and performance.
data.conclusion#Conclusion: The heterodyne architecture is a cornerstone of modern radio communication systems, thanks to its ability to simplify high-frequency signal processing while offering excellent selectivity and sensitivity. By combining components such as antenna, LNA, mixer, local oscillator and IF filters, it allows capturing and processing radio signals with great efficiency. This approach, although sometimes superseded by more recent architectures such as direct reception or software-defined radios (SDR), remains widely used in many applications, from classic AM/FM radios to advanced telecommunication systems. With the evolution of technologies, RF chains continue to adapt, integrating digital components and processing algorithms to meet growing needs in bandwidth and efficiency.
data.references#References: Razavi, B. (2011). RF Microelectronics (2nd ed.). Prentice Hall. Pozar, D. M. (2011). Microwave Engineering (4th ed.). Wiley. Armstrong, E. H. (1918). “The Super-Heterodyne—Its Origin, Development, and Some Recent Improvements.” Proceedings of the IRE. Haykin, S. (2001). Communication Systems (4th ed.). Wiley.
data.the-principle-of-heterodyne-in-radio#The:
The heterodyne, invented in the early 20th century by Reginald Fessenden and perfected by Edwin Armstrong, is a fundamental technique in radio frequency. It allows simplifying radio signal processing by converting a high-frequency RF signal to a lower intermediate frequency (IF). This conversion is achieved by mixing the RF signal with a reference signal generated by a local oscillator. The resulting intermediate frequency preserves the information of the original signal but is easier to amplify, filter and demodulate. For example, an RF signal at 100 MHz mixed with an LO signal at 90 MHz produces an IF at 10 MHz, which is simpler to manipulate with standard electronic circuits.
This technique is widely used in radio receivers, televisions and modern communication systems, as it allows reducing circuit complexity while improving selectivity and sensitivity.
data.the-principle-of-the-heterodyne-mixer#Th:
The mixer is at the heart of the heterodyne architecture. It performs a mathematical multiplication between two signals: the captured RF signal and the local oscillator signal. Consider the following signals:
RF Signal: sRF(t)=ARF⋅cos⁡(2πfRFt) s_{RF}(t) = A_{RF} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{RF}t) sRF​(t)=ARF​⋅cos(2πfRF​t) LO Signal: sLO(t)=ALO⋅cos⁡(2πfLOt) s_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{LO}t) sLO​(t)=ALO​⋅cos(2πfLO​t) The mixer produces the output signal:
sout(t)=sRF(t)⋅sLO(t) s_{out}(t) = s_{RF}(t) \cdot s_{LO}(t) sout​(t)=sRF​(t)⋅sLO​(t)Applying the trigonometric identity:
cos⁡(A)⋅cos⁡(B)=12[cos⁡(A+B)+cos⁡(A−B)] \cos(A) \cdot \cos(B) = \frac{1}{2} [\cos(A+B) + \cos(A-B)] cos(A)⋅cos(B)=21​[cos(A+B)+cos(A−B)]The resulting signal is:
sout(t)=ARFALO2[cos⁡(2π(fRF+fLO)t)+cos⁡(2π(fRF−fLO)t)] s_{out}(t) = \frac{A_{RF} A_{LO}}{2} [\cos(2\pi (f_{RF}+f_{LO})t) + \cos(2\pi (f_{RF}-f_{LO})t)] sout​(t)=2ARF​ALO​​[cos(2π(fRF​+fLO​)t)+cos(2π(fRF​−fLO​)t)]This result shows that the mixer generates two new frequencies:
The sum: fRF+fLO f_{RF} + f_{LO} fRF​+fLO​ The difference: ∣fRF−fLO∣ |f_{RF} - f_{LO}| ∣fRF​−fLO​∣ An IF filter generally selects the difference frequency, called intermediate frequency (fIF f_{IF} fIF​), because it is lower and easier to process. For example, for fRF=100 MHz f_{RF} = 100 \, \text{MHz} fRF​=100MHz and fLO=90 MHz f_{LO} = 90 \, \text{MHz} fLO​=90MHz, the IF is fIF=10 MHz f_{IF} = 10 \, \text{MHz} fIF​=10MHz.
In practical applications, balanced or doubly balanced mixers are often used to reduce interference and unwanted mixing products, such as harmonics or image signals.

data: Software-defined radio is based on a simple principle: replace most analog RF and IF processing with digital operations. The tipping point between these two worlds is signal digitization. It is at this precise moment that an analog signal, captured by the antenna and shaped by the RF front-end, becomes a series of samples exploitable by algorithms. The performance of an SDR is therefore directly linked to the quality of this analog-to-digital conversion.
data.acquisition-architectures-in-sdr#Acquisi:
The choice of acquisition architecture determines the front-end complexity and constraints imposed on the ADC. The heterodyne approach remains very widespread: the RF signal is first translated to a lower intermediate frequency using a mixer, then filtered before being digitized. This method allows reducing the required sampling frequency and using less expensive ADCs that are less sensitive to clock jitter. However, it requires multiplying analog stages, with associated drawbacks: intermodulation distortion, local oscillator phase noise, thermal drift and the need for regular calibrations.
At the opposite end, direct digitization, or direct sampling, consists of attacking the ADC directly in RF band. This solution eliminates the intermediate frequency and considerably simplifies the analog chain, while offering a very wide instantaneous band. However, it requires very fast converters, often at several giga-samples per second, whose cost, consumption and jitter sensitivity are much higher.
Diagram of different types of architectures. Between these two approaches, undersampling, or bandpass sampling, occupies a special place. It relies on deliberate spectral folding of an RF band into the Nyquist zone through precise choice of sampling frequency. This allows processing high-frequency signals with a slower ADC, but requires very selective input filtering to avoid unwanted superposition of other bands.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Digitization: reminders and deeper insights Acquisition architectures in SDR Critical parameters
data.conclusion#Conclusion: Signal digitization in SDR systems is a key process that relies on a balance between sampling frequency, resolution and management of constraints imposed by the RF environment. The Nyquist–Shannon theorem and spectral aliasing management define the sampling basics, while architectures like heterodyne, direct digitization or undersampling offer complementary approaches adapted to different needs. Each architecture presents advantages and challenges: heterodyne favors ADC simplicity at the cost of a complex analog chain, direct digitization maximizes flexibility at the cost of expensive converters, and undersampling optimizes resources provided precise filtering. Critical parameters, such as ENOB, SFDR, jitter and latency, play a determining role in overall performance, particularly in demanding applications like radar or modern communications. With the evolution of technologies, SDR systems continue to integrate increasingly powerful ADCs and advanced algorithms to meet the growing requirements of bandwidth, precision and real-time processing.
data.critical-parameters#Critical parameters:
Simply mentioning sampling frequency and number of bits is not sufficient to describe the real performance of an SDR chain. Other parameters directly influence the quality of the digitized signal. The ENOB, or Effective Number of Bits, expresses the effective resolution taking into account noise and distortion generated by the converter. It is always lower than the theoretical resolution and tends to decrease when the input frequency increases. The SFDR, or Spurious-Free Dynamic Range, measures the system’s ability to distinguish a weak signal in the presence of strong parasitic signals. In crowded RF environments, a high SFDR is crucial to prevent intermodulation products from masking signals of interest.
The sampling clock jitter also plays a major role, especially at high frequencies. An RMS jitter of 100 femtoseconds limits for example the SNR to about 70 dB for a 100 MHz signal, and to much less for a gigahertz signal. Finally, the total latency, that is, the delay between RF capture and availability of processed samples, can become critical in applications such as radar, real-time bidirectional communications or adaptive control systems. The overall dynamic range, which combines ADC resolution, SFDR and automatic gain management, determines the system’s ability to simultaneously process signals of very different levels without distortion or saturation.
data.digitization-reminders-and-deeper-insigh:
Analog-to-digital conversion first relies on sampling, that is, measuring the signal amplitude at regular intervals defined by a sampling frequency fe f_e fe​.
The Nyquist–Shannon theorem requires that fe f_e fe​ be at least twice the maximum frequency contained in the signal, failing which irreversible spectral aliasing will corrupt the information. In a radio context, it is sometimes possible to circumvent the apparent constraint of ultra-fast sampling by exploiting undersampling, or bandpass sampling. This technique consists of targeting a narrow RF band, even if located at high frequency, and choosing a sampling frequency that deliberately folds this band into the Nyquist zone, provided that anti-aliasing filtering is perfectly controlled.
Once the sampling instants are defined, the analog-to-digital converter proceeds to quantization, that is, assigning a digital value coded on a certain number of bits N N N to each measurement. The resolution thus obtained determines the theoretical signal-to-noise ratio related to quantization, given by the relation
SNRquant=6.02N+1.76 dB SNR_{quant} = 6.02N + 1.76 \, \text{dB} SNRquant​=6.02N+1.76dBEach additional bit doubles the precision and increases the SNR by about 6 dB, but also generates a larger volume of data. Indeed, the raw data rate to process equals the product of fe f_e fe​ by N N N. For example, a stream of 250 mega-samples per second on 14 bits already represents 3.5 gigabits per second, which requires the downstream FPGA or DSP to have sufficient internal bandwidth to process this data in real time.

data: Software-defined radio (SDR) owes its flexibility to the fact that most modulation, demodulation and filtering operations are performed digitally. But to efficiently manipulate an RF signal in the digital domain, one must have a representation that preserves the entirety of amplitude and phase information. This is precisely the role of IQ architecture: providing the processor with a complex signal, composed of an “In phase” (I) part and a “Quadrature” (Q) part, perfectly synchronized and phase-shifted by 90°. This representation, inherited from complex signal theory, has become essential in SDR.
data.advantages-of-iq-representation#Advantag: Using a complex baseband signal allows simultaneous representation of positive and negative spectral components without ambiguity, which doubles the useful bandwidth compared to a real signal sampled at the same frequency. Complex modulations like QPSK, QAM, OFDM or any vector modulation are naturally expressed in this formalism. Furthermore, frequency shifts translate simply to complex multiplication, which avoids additional analog operations and makes SDR intrinsically more flexible.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Origin and principle of IQ representation Hardware implementation of IQ architecture Advantages of IQ representation Limitations and real imperfections Place of IQ in the SDR chain
data.conclusion#Conclusion: IQ architecture is much more than a simple representation convention: it is a necessary condition to fully exploit the power of digital processing in SDR. By simultaneously providing signal amplitude and phase, it makes possible direct manipulation of complex spectra, implementation of sophisticated modulations and digital correction of hardware imperfections. Mastery of this architecture, both theoretically and practically, is an essential prerequisite for any engineer working on the design or operation of modern SDR systems.
data.hardware-implementation-of-iq-architectu:
In reception, IQ stream generation relies on a quadrature mixer. The input RF signal is first divided into two identical paths. The first is multiplied by a local cosine wave, generated by the local oscillator (LO). The second is multiplied by a local sine wave, obtained by introducing a precise 90° phase shift on the LO. The mixing products contain the sum and difference of input and LO frequencies; low-pass filtering preserves only the intermediate frequency component close to zero. Each filtered path is then digitized by an independent analog-to-digital converter, thus providing two parallel streams: I[n] and Q[n].
In transmission, the path is symmetrical: the digital I and Q streams generated by the processor pass through digital-to-analog converters (DAC), then are recombined by a quadrature mixer to reconstruct a real RF signal.
Internal architecture of the AD9361
data.limitations-and-real-imperfections#Limit:
In practice, generating perfect I and Q channels is impossible. Small amplitude imbalances between the two channels, phase errors relative to the ideal 90°, or nonlinearities introduce imperfect image rejection. These defects manifest as the presence of an unwanted mirror signal symmetrical relative to the center frequency. For example, a phase error of 2° or an imbalance of 0.5 dB can limit image rejection to less than 40 dB. In modern SDR, these defects can however be mitigated by digital calibration algorithms, often executed automatically at startup or continuously.
Real imperfections of I/Q architecture
data.origin-and-principle-of-iq-representatio:
A pure RF signal can be described by a sinusoid:
s(t)=Acos⁡(ωt+ϕ) s(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)s(t)=Acos(ωt+ϕ)In practice, a modulated signal occupies a certain band around the carrier ω and contains amplitude and phase variations. To process it efficiently, it is preferable to represent it as a combination of two orthogonal components:
s(t)=I(t)cos⁡(ωt)−Q(t)sin⁡(ωt) s(t) = I (t) \cos(\omega t) - Q(t) \sin (\omega t)s(t)=I(t)cos(ωt)−Q(t)sin(ωt)where I(t) and Q(t) are two low-frequency signals carrying respectively the real and imaginary parts of a complex version of the signal. In this notation, the signal becomes:
sc=I(t)+jQ(t) s_{c} = I(t) + j Q(t)sc​=I(t)+jQ(t)This approach allows transposing modulated signals to complex baseband, where processing operations (frequency shifting, filtering, demodulation) are mathematically simple.
I/Q architecture in reception
data.place-of-iq-in-the-sdr-chain#Place of IQ: In a direct sampling SDR architecture, the ADC output can already provide an IQ stream if the conversion architecture integrates it internally. In heterodyne architectures, IQ generation occurs after one or several intermediate frequency changes, but before purely digital processing. In all cases, it is this IQ stream that is transmitted to the FPGA, DSP or host processor to be analyzed, filtered, demodulated or retransmitted.

Collins, A. A., et al. (2018). Software Defined Radio with GNU Radio and USRP. Wiley. Reed, J. H. (2002). Software Radio: A Systems Approach. Prentice Hall. GNU Radio Project. (2023). GNU Radio Documentation and Tutorials. Available at: https://wiki.gnuradio.org. Proakis, J. G., & Salehi, M. (2008). Digital Communications (5th ed.). McGraw-Hill.

data: Software-defined radio (SDR) represents a revolution in the field of radio communications by replacing traditional analog circuits with digital algorithms executed on processors, DSPs or FPGAs. When a radio signal is captured by an antenna, it is digitized by an analog-to-digital converter (ADC), producing a stream of complex samples (I/Q) that enters a digital processing chain. This chain transforms raw data into exploitable information, such as audio for an FM station, data for a Wi-Fi network, or navigation signals for GPS. The power of SDR lies in its flexibility: the same hardware can be reconfigured by software to process different protocols or modulations, from FM broadcasting at 98 MHz to LTE signal reception, without requiring hardware modifications. This flexibility relies on a carefully orchestrated sequence of digital operations: filtering to eliminate interference, sampling rate adjustment to optimize calculations, frequency translation to center the signal, channel extraction to isolate the useful band, equalization to correct distortions, and management of latency and throughput constraints for real-time processing.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Digital filtering: FIR and IIR Decimation and interpolation Frequency translation (digital mixing) Channel extraction Equalization Latency and processing throughput Conclusion
data.channel-extraction#Channel extraction: Once the signal is centered in baseband, channel extraction allows isolating the bandwidth of interest, such as a 200 kHz FM channel or a 1.536 MHz DAB+ channel, from the digitized spectrum. This step combines selective filtering, often performed with an FIR filter for its precision, and decimation to adapt the sampling rate to the channel bandwidth. For example, an SDR receiver capturing a wide spectrum at 10 Msps can extract a DAB+ channel by applying a low-pass filter followed by decimation to 2 Msps, thus reducing the amount of data to process. This operation ensures that only relevant samples are transmitted to subsequent steps, optimizing system efficiency. In applications such as Wi-Fi signal reception or digital television, channel extraction is a key step for isolating a specific signal among several simultaneously captured channels.
data.conclusion#Conclusion: The digital processing chain in a software-defined radio transforms a raw digitized signal into exploitable information through a logical and well-defined sequence: filtering eliminates interference, decimation and interpolation optimize sampling rate, frequency translation centers the signal in baseband, channel extraction isolates the useful band, equalization corrects distortions, and latency and throughput management guarantees real-time feasibility. Each step plays a precise role, relying on sophisticated algorithms to offer the flexibility that characterizes SDR. Whether receiving an FM station, decoding a Wi-Fi signal or experimenting with amateur radio protocols, these operations form the foundation of a high-performance SDR.
data.decimation-and-interpolation#Decimation: After initial filtering, it is often necessary to adjust the signal’s sampling frequency to reduce computational load or prepare specific processing. Decimation consists of decreasing this frequency, for example going from 2 Msps to 200 kilosamples per second (ksps) to process an FM channel. Simple sample suppression would cause aliasing, where out-of-band frequencies fold into the useful spectrum, degrading information. To avoid this, a low-pass filter is applied before decimation, with a cutoff frequency lower than half the new sampling frequency, in accordance with the Nyquist-Shannon theorem. Conversely, interpolation increases the sampling rate by inserting calculated samples, generally zeros, followed by low-pass filtering to smooth the signal. For example, an SDR capturing a wide spectrum at 20 Msps can decimate to 200 ksps to isolate a 20 MHz Wi-Fi channel, thus reducing computation requirements for subsequent steps. These techniques are essential for adapting the data flow to processing algorithm constraints, such as in wideband receivers where multiple channels must be efficiently isolated. They also allow preparing the signal for specific modulations or transmissions, optimizing hardware resource utilization.
data.digital-filtering-fir-and-iir#Digital fi:
Once digitized by the ADC, the raw signal often contains interference from frequencies outside the band of interest, such as adjacent signals or noise. Digital filtering constitutes the first major step to clean this signal and limit its bandwidth to that necessary for subsequent processing. For example, in an SDR capturing a wide spectrum at 2 megasamples per second (Msps), a filter can isolate a 200 kHz FM channel. Two types of filters dominate in this context: finite impulse response (FIR) and infinite impulse response (IIR) filters. FIR filters calculate their output by convolution, where each output sample is a linear combination of input samples weighted by predefined coefficients, according to the equation: y[n]=∑k=0N−1h[k]x[n−k] y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k]x[n-k] y[n]=k=0∑N−1​h[k]x[n−k]The FIR filter architecture is as follows:
FIR filter architecture This method guarantees absolute stability, as there is no feedback, and linear phase, essential for preserving signal shape in complex modulations like QAM. For example, a 64-coefficient FIR filter can be designed to isolate a 1.536 MHz DAB+ channel with high precision. In contrast, IIR filters integrate feedback, where the output depends on both current inputs and previous outputs, following an equation of the form: y[n]=∑k=0Nb[k]x[n−k]−∑k=1Ma[k]y[n−k] y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N} b[k]x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{M} a[k]y[n-k]y[n]=k=0∑N​b[k]x[n−k]−k=1∑M​a[k]y[n−k]The IIR filter architecture is as follows:
IIR filter architecture They offer high selectivity with fewer coefficients, which reduces computational load, but their nonlinear phase and risk of instability make them less suitable for critical applications. In an SDR, FIR filters are often preferred for precise reception, such as in digital broadcasting systems, while IIR are used for fast pre-filters or applications where phase is less critical.
data.equalization#Equalization: The radio propagation channel often introduces distortions, such as selective attenuation of certain frequencies or interference due to echoes, which degrade signal quality. Equalization compensates for these effects to restore signal integrity before demodulation. Fixed equalizers rely on prior channel estimation, often obtained via pilot signals, as in OFDM systems used for Wi-Fi or 4G. Adaptive equalizers, on the other hand, adjust their coefficients in real time using algorithms like LMS (Least Mean Squares), particularly useful in changing environments, such as Wi-Fi reception in urban environments with multiple reflections. For example, an adaptive equalizer can correct inter-symbol interference in a QAM signal, ensuring a clear constellation for reliable demodulation. This step is crucial for complex modulations where distortions can compromise data recovery.
data.frequency-translation-digital-mixing#Fre: Frequency translation, or digital mixing, plays a crucial role by moving the signal in the spectrum to center it around 0 Hz, in baseband, to simplify subsequent processing. In an analog radio, a mixer combines the signal with a sinusoid generated by a local oscillator. Digitally, this operation is performed by multiplying samples by a complex sinusoid, produced by a numerical oscillator (NCO), according to the equation: x′[n]=x[n]⋅e−j2πf0n/fs x'[n] = x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_0 n / f_s} x′[n]=x[n]⋅e−j2πf0​n/fs​, where f0 f_0 f0​ is the translation frequency and fs f_s fs​ the sampling frequency. For example, an FM signal initially centered at 1 MHz after a first analog mix can be brought back to 0 Hz to facilitate audio extraction. This step is essential for isolating a specific channel in a wide spectrum, such as an LTE signal in a 20 MHz band, and reduces demodulation algorithm complexity. NCO precision is critical to avoid frequency errors.
data.latency-and-processing-throughput#Latenc: The performance of a digital processing chain in an SDR depends on two critical constraints: latency and processing throughput. Latency represents the delay between signal input into the chain and its processed output, influenced by factors such as filter length or processing block size. For example, a 64-coefficient FIR filter at 2 Msps introduces a delay of about 32 microseconds, which can be problematic in real-time applications like VoIP telephony. Processing throughput, meanwhile, measures the amount of data processed per second. A 10 Msps signal requires processing 10 million complex samples per second, which may necessitate specialized processors like DSPs or FPGAs to meet computational requirements. For example, a 128-coefficient FIR filter on a 2 Msps signal requires about 256 million operations per second (MFLOPS), a load that a modern PC can handle, but which exceeds the capabilities of a basic microcontroller. These constraints are essential for guaranteeing real-time processing, particularly in applications such as broadcasting or cellular communications.

data: Phase-locked loops, known by the acronym PLL (Phase Locked Loop), constitute the invisible backbone of our modern electronic world. From smartphones to satellites, including the most sophisticated metrology equipment, these ingenious circuits silently orchestrate the generation and synchronization of millions of signals. Understanding their operation means grasping one of the fundamental mechanisms that allow our communication systems to function with remarkable precision.
data.conclusion#Conclusion:
Phase-locked loops embody the elegance of electronic engineering: a simple principle - phase feedback - which, properly implemented, solves complex problems of signal generation and synchronization. From the discrete analog PLL of the 1930s to today’s integrated fractional synthesizers, this technology has continuously evolved to meet the ever-increasing demands of communication systems.
In the context of software-defined radio that we explore in this series of articles, PLLs play a fundamental role by providing the temporal and frequency references essential for the proper functioning of converters and digital processing algorithms. Understanding their operation allows better appreciation of the performance and limitations of modern SDR systems, where the quality of frequency synthesis directly conditions the receiver’s sensitivity, selectivity, and dynamic range.
data.non-unity-feedback-phase-locked-loop#Non:
The non-unity feedback architecture transforms the PLL into a true programmable frequency synthesizer. By inserting a frequency divider in the feedback loop, the VCO is forced to oscillate at an exact multiple of the reference frequency, thus opening the way to generating thousands of different frequencies from a single reference.
Block diagram of a non-unity feedback phase-locked loop The principle relies on an elegant trick: the phase detector compares the reference not directly to the VCO signal, but to this signal divided by a programmable factor N. At equilibrium, the two compared signals must have the same frequency. If the reference oscillates at fref, the VCO must therefore oscillate at N × fref so that, after division, the frequencies are identical at the comparator level. By simply modifying the value of N, the output frequency is instantly changed in steps of fref.
This architecture offers remarkable flexibility for modern radiocommunication systems. A transceiver covering the 88-108 MHz band with channels spaced 100 kHz apart can use a 100 kHz reference and vary N from 880 to 1080. The frequency resolution equals the reference frequency, allowing any channel to be reached. However, this resolution imposes a compromise: a low-frequency reference offers fine resolution but slows the loop response, while a high reference accelerates the loop but limits resolution.
Modern fractional PLLs circumvent this limitation by using dividers capable of non-integer ratios. Through sophisticated sigma-delta modulation techniques, they can generate frequencies with resolution much finer than the reference frequency, while maintaining high loop bandwidth. These architectures allow ultra-fast switching times, essential for frequency-hopping systems or cognitive radios that must rapidly explore the spectrum.
Increasing integration has given birth to remarkable monolithic PLL circuits. A modern synthesizer integrates on a few square millimeters of silicon all loop elements, including multi-band VCOs covering several gigahertz, high-speed programmable dividers, digital phase detectors, and even adaptive loop filters.
data.objective-of-the-phase-locked-loop#Objec:
A PLL pursues an apparently simple but technically ambitious objective: to generate an output signal whose phase remains perfectly synchronized with that of a reference signal. This phase synchronization naturally implies frequency equality, transforming the PLL into a true programmable frequency synthesizer.
The principle relies on a negative feedback loop that continuously compares the phase of the generated signal with that of a reference. Any drift is immediately detected and corrected, thus maintaining precise locking. This locking capability offers three major advantages. First, it allows multiplying or dividing a reference frequency with extreme precision, thus creating signals at precise frequencies different from the reference. Second, it effectively filters phase noise, producing signals of remarkable spectral purity. Finally, it ensures long-term stability directly inherited from the reference, generally a high-quality quartz oscillator.
In the context of radiocommunications, PLLs fulfill critical functions. They generate the carriers necessary for transmitting modulated signals and create the local oscillators essential for frequency conversion stages in reception. They also participate in the demodulation of FM and FSK signals, exploiting the VCO control voltage as a demodulated signal. In digital systems, they recover the clock embedded in transmitted data, allowing perfect synchronization between transmitter and receiver.

Plenum Press. Rusek, F. (2010). Carrier and Symbol Timing Recovery for SDR. IEEE Transactions on Communications, 58(3), 785-795. GNU Radio Community. (2023). GNU Radio Manual and Documentation. Available at: https://www.gnuradio.org. Rice, M. (2009). Digital Communications: A Discrete-Time Approach. Pearson Prentice Hall.
data.stable-frequency-signal-requirements-in-:
A modern radio receiver, whether it’s a classic superheterodyne or based on an SDR architecture, requires a multitude of reference signals of impeccable stability. This requirement stems directly from the physical and technical constraints imposed by radioelectric signal processing.
Take the example of a superheterodyne receiver tuned to 100 MHz with a channel bandwidth of 25 kHz. The local oscillator must maintain its frequency with precision better than ±1 kHz to ensure that the signal remains centered in the intermediate frequency filter. This represents a relative stability of 10 ppm (parts per million), already difficult to achieve with a simple LC oscillator. For modern digital communications, where channels may be spaced by only a few kilohertz, requirements become even stricter, requiring stabilities of the order of 1 ppm or better.
Frequency drift of free-running oscillators constitutes a major problem. A classic LC oscillator can drift by several kilohertz over a temperature range of 50°C, making stable reception of a narrow-band signal impossible. Supply voltage variations, component aging, and mechanical vibrations further aggravate this instability. Even quartz oscillators, though renowned for their stability, require sophisticated temperature compensation to achieve the required performance.
This is where the PLL reveals all its power. By locking a variable oscillator (VCO) to a stable reference, it combines the best of both worlds: the frequency agility of the VCO and the long-term stability of the reference. A modern receiver can thus use a single high-quality quartz reference to generate, via several PLLs, all the signals necessary for its operation: local oscillators for different conversion stages, sampling clock for analog-to-digital converters, processing frequencies for digital signal processors.
data.unity-feedback-phase-locked-loop#Unity f:
The unity feedback PLL represents the most elementary form of phase-locked loop, where the output signal is directly fed back to the phase detector without modification. This architecture, although simple, perfectly illustrates the fundamental mechanisms of phase locking.
At the heart of this loop, three essential elements interact permanently. The phase detector compares the instantaneous phases of the reference signal and output signal, producing an error voltage proportional to their difference. This voltage then passes through a loop filter, generally a low-pass filter, which determines the system’s response dynamics. The filter eliminates unwanted high-frequency components while defining the loop bandwidth. Finally, the voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) generates the output signal whose frequency varies linearly with the control voltage from the filter.
The locking process establishes itself progressively. Initially, if the frequencies differ, the phase detector produces a beat at the difference frequency. This low-frequency component, after filtering, modulates the VCO which progressively approaches the reference frequency. When the gap becomes sufficiently small, the loop “locks” and maintains stable locking. The VCO phase then faithfully follows that of the reference, with a constant offset determined by the loop characteristics.
The design of the loop filter is of paramount importance. A first-order filter, simple parallel capacitance, offers stable response but a relatively long acquisition time. Second-order filters, integrating resistances and capacitances, allow optimization of the compromise between acquisition speed and stability. The filter bandwidth directly defines the PLL’s capture range, i.e., the maximum initial frequency deviation allowing locking, as well as the loop’s ability to follow rapid variations in the reference.
Block diagram of a phase-locked loop

data: Digital modulations and demodulations constitute an essential element of modern telecommunications, enabling efficient transmission of binary information through different communication channels.
data.amplitude-modulations#Amplitude modulati:
Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) represents the simplest form of digital modulation. In binary ASK, two distinct amplitudes correspond to bits 0 and 1. Generally, the absence of carrier (zero amplitude) represents bit 0, while a given amplitude represents bit 1. This technique, although simple to implement, presents significant sensitivity to noise and channel amplitude variations.
Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) combines amplitude and phase modulations, allowing high spectral efficiencies to be achieved. In QAM, symbols are positioned in the complex plane according to a regular geometric constellation. QAM-16, QAM-64 or QAM-256 modulations transmit respectively 4, 6 or 8 bits per symbol, thus multiplying the data rate without widening the bandwidth.
data.article-plan#Article Plan: Fundamental principles of digital modulation Amplitude modulations Frequency modulations Phase modulations Demodulation process
data.conclusion#Conclusion: This article has explored digital modulation and demodulation techniques, fundamental elements of modern telecommunications. These methods enable efficient transmission of binary data by optimizing radio spectrum utilization. The different approaches (ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM) offer solutions adapted to the specific constraints of each application, balancing robustness, spectral efficiency and implementation complexity for the evolution of communication systems.
data.demodulation-process#Demodulation proces:
Digital demodulation recovers the original binary data from the received modulated signal. This complex process comprises several critical steps: carrier synchronization, symbol synchronization, channel equalization, and decision on received symbols.
Coherent demodulation requires reconstruction of the carrier in phase with that of the transmitter. Phase-locked loops (PLL) and carrier recovery algorithms (Costas loop) enable this synchronization. The precision of this step directly influences demodulation performance, particularly for phase modulations.
Differential demodulation avoids the need for absolute carrier recovery by encoding information in transitions between consecutive symbols. Although less sensitive to phase errors, this technique generally presents poorer performance in terms of bit error rate.
data.frequency-modulations#Frequency modulati:
Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) uses different carrier frequencies to represent binary symbols. In binary FSK, two distinct frequencies f1 f_1 f1​ and f2 f_2 f2​ correspond to bits 0 and 1. The spacing between these frequencies, called frequency deviation, directly influences performance in terms of bit error rate and spectral occupancy.
Continuous Phase FSK (CPFSK) improves spectral efficiency by maintaining phase continuity between symbol transitions. Gaussian FSK (GFSK), used notably in GSM systems, applies Gaussian filtering to binary data before FSK modulation, thus reducing spectral side lobes and interference between adjacent channels.
data.fundamental-principles-of-digital-modula:
Digital modulation consists of modifying the characteristics of a sinusoidal carrier (amplitude, frequency or phase) according to the binary data to be transmitted. Unlike analog modulation where the modulating signal is continuous, digital modulation processes discrete bit sequences, generally organized into symbols.
The process begins with the mapping of information bits onto symbols, each symbol being able to represent one or several bits depending on the modulation complexity. These symbols are then used to modify the carrier according to the chosen technique. Spectral efficiency, measured in bits per second per hertz, constitutes a crucial parameter determining the amount of information transmittable in a given frequency band.
data.phase-modulations#Phase modulations:
Phase Shift Keying (PSK) modifies the carrier phase according to the data to be transmitted. In binary PSK (BPSK), two opposite phases (0° and 180°) represent bits 0 and 1. This technique offers excellent noise robustness, particularly suited to applications requiring high reliability.
Quaternary PSK (QPSK) uses four equally spaced phases (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) allowing transmission of 2 bits per symbol. Each QPSK symbol corresponds to a bit pair, thus doubling spectral efficiency compared to BPSK without increasing the necessary bandwidth.
Higher-order PSK modulations (8-PSK, 16-PSK) increase the number of bits per symbol but at the cost of increased sensitivity to noise and phase distortions. The Euclidean distance between constellation points decreases with increasing modulation order, degrading performance in the presence of noise.

data: Software-defined radio (SDR) tools today constitute a rich and diverse technological ecosystem that is revolutionizing the traditional approach to radio communications. These solutions, combining specialized hardware with sophisticated software, transform the physical constraints of conventional radio architectures into programmable digital flexibility, opening new opportunities for experimentation, research, and professional applications.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: Classification of SDR Tools SDR Hardware: From Entry-Level to Professional Classification of SDR Tools SDR Hardware: From Entry-Level to Professional Software and Development Frameworks
data.classification-of-sdr-tools#Classificati:
The SDR tools universe is structured according to several classification axes reflecting the diversity of needs and applications. The fundamental distinction separates hardware solutions from software, each fulfilling complementary roles in the radio processing chain.
SDR hardware tools include analog-to-digital conversion devices, RF front-ends, and integrated processing platforms. These components provide the critical interface between the analog world of radio signals and the digital environment where software processing occurs. Their performance directly determines the system’s capabilities in terms of bandwidth, sensitivity, and frequency range.
Software tools include development frameworks, reception and analysis applications, and signal processing libraries. This software layer gives SDR systems their characteristic flexibility, allowing radio functionalities to be modified through code reconfiguration rather than hardware replacement.
A secondary classification differentiates tools according to their complexity level and cost. Entry-level solutions, accessible to hobbyists and students, coexist with professional platforms designed for advanced research and critical applications. This stratification encourages gradual adoption of SDR technology across different user segments.
data.conclusion#Conclusion:
The SDR tools ecosystem perfectly illustrates the convergence between technological innovation and democratization of access to advanced technologies. This silent revolution is gradually transforming the radio communications landscape, from amateur experimentation to the most critical industrial applications. The diversity of available solutions, from entry-level devices to sophisticated professional platforms, demonstrates the maturity this technology has reached. The constant evolution of hardware performance and software sophistication suggests a promising future for SDR tools. The increasing integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning capabilities opens new perspectives for automatic optimization of radio systems and dynamic adaptation to propagation conditions.
The formation of a global community of developers and users is one of the major strengths of the SDR ecosystem. This open collaboration accelerates innovation and ensures the sustainability of developed solutions. The emergence of common standards and standardized interfaces will facilitate interoperability and reduce adoption barriers.
data.entry-level-solutions#Entry-Level Soluti:
The RTL-SDR represents the archetype of entry-level solutions that have democratized access to SDR technology. Based on the RTL2832U chipset originally designed for digital TV reception, this USB device offers a frequency range from 24 MHz to 1766 MHz at a cost under 40 euros. Its ease of use and excellent value make it the tool of choice for learning SDR concepts and radio experimentation.
RTL-SDR RTL-SDR performance, although limited by its 8-bit resolution and 2.4 MHz bandwidth, is more than sufficient for many educational and experimental applications. Reception of FM broadcast signals, aeronautical communications, and telemetry protocols illustrate the diversity of possible uses with this accessible equipment.
The SDRplay RSP1A extends entry-level capabilities with improved 12-bit resolution and an extended frequency range from 1 kHz to 2 GHz. This technological evolution provides higher sensitivity and better dynamic range, particularly appreciated for weak signal reception and fine spectral analysis.
SDRplay
data.gnu-radio-the-open-source-standard#GNU R:
GNU Radio is the reference framework for SDR application development. This open-source platform provides an extensive library of signal processing blocks, facilitating the implementation of complex algorithms through graphical assembly of functional modules. The graphical approach of GNU Radio Companion (GRC) democratizes access to advanced signal processing techniques.
GNU Radio’s modular architecture relies on a system of interconnected blocks via typed data streams. This organization promotes code reuse and simplifies debugging of complex applications. Basic blocks cover all common operations: filtering, modulation, demodulation, synchronization, and visualization.
GNURadio GNU Radio’s extensibility allows integration of custom blocks developed in C++ or Python. This flexibility permits implementation of proprietary algorithms while benefiting from the robust infrastructure of the framework. The active community regularly contributes new specialized blocks, continuously enriching the ecosystem.
data.integrated-development-environments#Inte:
SDR++ has recently emerged as a modern solution combining performance and ease of use. Developed in C++ with a plugin architecture, it offers a solid foundation for developing specialized SDR applications. Its modular interface allows adding features without modifying the main code.
Universal Radio Hacker (URH) specializes in analyzing unknown wireless protocols. This application facilitates reverse-engineering of proprietary communications through advanced visualization tools and automatic analysis algorithms. Its intuitive interface guides the user in decoding complex protocols.
MATLAB and Simulink integrate native SDR toolboxes for rapid prototyping. These commercial environments offer high performance and seamless integration with simulation and testing tools. Automatic code generation allows direct deployment of developed algorithms to target hardware platforms.
data.intermediate-platforms#Intermediate Plat:
The HackRF One occupies a unique position as a bidirectional platform capable of transmitting and receiving over an exceptionally wide range from 1 MHz to 6 GHz. With its 8-bit resolution and 20 MHz bandwidth, it is a versatile tool for learning modulation techniques and experimenting with radio transmission.
SDR HackRF Analog Devices’ ADALM-Pluto brings AD9363 technology in a compact and affordable format. This platform covers 325-3800 MHz with 12-bit resolution and configurable bandwidth up to 20 MHz. Its native integration with MATLAB and GNU Radio facilitates rapid prototyping of advanced signal processing applications.
Pluto SDR The LimeSDR stands out with its fully open-source architecture and high performance. Based on the LMS7002M chipset, it offers frequency coverage from 100 kHz to 3.8 GHz with 12-bit resolution and adjustable bandwidth up to 61.44 MHz. This technical flexibility makes it a preferred platform for academic research and specialized application development.
Lime SDR
data.professional-solutions#Professional Solu:
Ettus Research’s USRP (Universal Software Radio Peripheral) platforms set the standard for professional SDR tools. The USRP range offers modular solutions adapted to the strictest requirements in terms of performance, reliability, and flexibility. USRP B200/B210 models offer intermediate performance, while the N and X310 series achieve industrial-grade specifications.
USRP The modular architecture of USRPs allows adaptation to specific needs by selecting daughterboards covering different frequency bands. This modular approach optimizes performance for each application while preserving investment in the base platform. USRP solutions integrate programmable FPGAs enabling implementation of critical real-time processing algorithms. This onboard processing capability reduces latency and frees host processor resources for higher-level tasks.

data.specialized-reception-applications#Speci:
Gqrx has established itself as the reference SDR reception application for end-users. Based on GNU Radio and the Qt interface, it offers an intuitive user interface for listening to and analyzing radio signals. Features include real-time spectral visualization, audio recording, and support for a wide range of SDR hardware.
SDR# (SDRSharp) offers a native Windows alternative with a sleek interface and optimized performance. This application supports numerous SDR devices and integrates plugins for specialized functionalities such as digital mode decoding and advanced spectral analysis.
CubicSDR provides a cross-platform approach with an innovative 3D interface for spectral visualization. Its OpenGL architecture leverages graphics acceleration to display smooth spectrograms even with high data rates.

data: Refrigerators now send notifications to smartphones. Thermostats adapt to occupants’ habits. Smartwatches monitor heart rates in real time. These devices form the Internet of Things (IoT). Each device functions as a small connected computer. The IoT revolution is transforming daily life while introducing new security risks. Every sensor represents a potential entry point for attackers. Securing these billions of devices has become essential. This article series explores the challenges of IoT security.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: European Regulatory Framework: RED and EN Standards for IoT IoT Secure Development Process Identification of Critical Assets Understanding IoT Threats Implementation of Security Requirements Validation and Maintenance of Security
data.conclusion#Conclusion: The Internet of Things integrates computing into daily life, offering significant benefits but also exposing risks if vulnerabilities proliferate. Each connected sensor is a critical link in our collective security. The European regulatory framework is progressing positively. The RED Directive transforms recommendations into strict legal obligations, marking a turning point for the IoT industry. Security is becoming a competitive advantage in the market. A secure development methodology tailored to IoT constraints provides a proven framework. Although the initial investment in security may seem high, the cost of inaction grows exponentially with deployment scale. Future articles in this series will explore practical aspects of IoT security, detailing the selection of cryptographic components, network architecture, and secure update mechanisms. The future of IoT depends on current technical choices. Each designed device either strengthens a secure IoT ecosystem or contributes to an uncontrollable attack surface. Developers’ expertise plays a pivotal role in building an inherently secure IoT.
data.en-standards-guide-secure-implementation: European EN standards translate legal obligations into practical technical specifications to guide the implementation of secure solutions. The EN 303 645 standard focuses on the security of consumer IoT devices. It defines thirteen fundamental requirements, such as prohibiting universal default passwords, mandating secure updates, reducing exposed services, and requiring encryption for all communications. For instance, a surveillance camera must have a unique password per device, automatic updates, and minimal open network ports. The EN 303 417 standard addresses modern radio communications, defining requirements for 5G and WiFi 6. The EN 62443 standard applies to industrial IoT, addressing aspects such as network segmentation and centralized identity management.
data.identification-of-critical-iot-assets#Id: The first step involves identifying all assets that require protection, going beyond a simple technical inventory. Hardware components include microcontrollers that process sensitive data, sensors that collect personal information, actuators that execute critical commands, and memory that stores programs and cryptographic keys. Data is particularly sensitive. Telemetry data can reveal user behaviors, connection metadata exposes network architecture, cryptographic keys secure the ecosystem, and authentication certificates validate legitimate identities. Intangible assets play a significant role. Protecting user privacy is a priority, service availability impacts user experience, reliability strengthens brand reputation, and regulatory compliance prevents costly penalties. Criticality assessment accounts for IoT-specific factors. For example, a temperature sensor in a greenhouse has lower criticality than a connected medical device. This detailed analysis enables prioritization of protection measures.
data.implementation-of-iot-security-requireme: Translating needs into technical requirements for IoT demands an approach tailored to its constraints. Balancing energy performance, production costs, and security involves complex trade-offs. Security requirements must be precise. For example, strong authentication may translate to mutual authentication based on ECC-256 certificates, renewed automatically every 30 days, with a fallback mechanism for connectivity loss. The security architecture must account for energy constraints. Each cryptographic operation consumes energy, necessitating a balance between security and efficiency. Dedicated cryptographic chips and optimized communication protocols reduce this consumption. Scalability poses a major challenge. The infrastructure must manage cryptographic keys and authentication certificates for thousands of connected devices while remaining reliable. Hardware constraints require rigorous optimization. For instance, an ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontroller with 32 KB of flash memory and 4 KB of RAM demands meticulous code optimization. Modern IoT frameworks, such as Zephyr, FreeRTOS, or Mbed OS, facilitate secure implementation by integrating optimized security mechanisms and proven protocols, reducing development complexity. Cryptographic key management is a critical technical challenge. Secure elements, like Atmel’s ATECC608 chips, provide hardware-based protection against physical attacks, though their integration slightly increases costs.
data.iot-secure-development-process#IoT Secur: Secure development of IoT devices relies on a methodology tailored to their specific constraints. These devices, often limited in energy and computing resources, must meet high security standards while operating in large-scale deployments where maintenance can be complex. This methodology is divided into six main phases. Each phase considers IoT-specific characteristics, balancing security, performance, and hardware constraints to ensure reliable and efficient solutions.
data.references#References: Anderson, R., & Moore, T. (2006). The Economics of Information Security. Science, 314(5799), 610-613. Sicari, S., Rizzardi, A., Grieco, L. A., & Coen-Porisini, A. (2015). Security, Privacy and Trust in Internet of Things. Computer Communications, 51, 146-164. ENISA. (2017). data.the-red-directive-strengthens-iot-securi: The European Union enhances the cybersecurity of connected devices through the RED Directive 2014/53/EU, which redefines market requirements. Article 3.3(d) represents a key milestone: every IoT device must incorporate protections against fraud and ensure the security of personal data. For example, a temperature sensor must demonstrate compliance to be marketed in Europe. This reform addresses a major issue. The rapid growth of IoT has often prioritized low costs over security, resulting in weak passwords, unencrypted communications, and a lack of updates. The RED Directive puts an end to these risky practices.
data.understanding-iot-threats#Understanding: Due to their unique characteristics, IoT devices are vulnerable to sophisticated and varied threats. IoT botnets are among the most visible threats. Attackers compromise millions of connected devices, turning them into zombies to launch massive DDoS attacks. For example, the 2016 Mirai attack infected 600,000 devices, generating traffic of 1 Tbps. IoT espionage is particularly concerning. Voice assistants continuously record, cameras monitor behaviors, and motion sensors track movements with precision. A compromised device allows attackers to access sensitive data, enabling intrusive surveillance. Physical attacks exploit the accessibility of IoT devices, often deployed without sufficient physical protection, which expands the attack surface. Denial-of-service attacks target energy constraints. By forcing battery-powered devices to perform intensive computations, attackers deplete their energy, rendering the devices inoperable.
data.validation-and-maintenance-of-iot-securi: IoT security testing requires tailored approaches, combining standard software checks with analyses of vulnerabilities related to energy and environmental constraints. Energy consumption tests under attack reveal specific vulnerabilities. An attacker can force a sensor to perform intensive computations, rapidly draining its battery and rendering the device unusable. Network robustness tests are critical. IoT devices often operate with unstable connections and limited bandwidth. Authentication mechanisms must remain operational after hours of disconnection. Physical security tests are essential. IoT devices, often physically accessible, require intrusion tests to verify whether attackers can extract cryptographic keys by tampering with the hardware. Scalability tests simulate real-world conditions. The security infrastructure must manage thousands of connected devices simultaneously in realistic scenarios. Security maintenance is complex, as devices may remain deployed for decades in hard-to-access environments. Vulnerability monitoring requires an automated approach to correlate newly published vulnerabilities with affected devices, enabling rapid risk identification. Large-scale secure updates are a complex challenge. Deploying a patch across 100,000 geographically dispersed smart meters requires sophisticated and secure orchestration.
